text: The surgeon incises the peritoneum three centimeters lateral to the colonic reflection and continues dissection through the retroperitoneal fat, revealing the transversus abdominis muscle.

The surgeon carefully dissects within the avascular plane, extending the dissection cranially onto the diaphragm by six to seven centimeters and caudally into the pelvis past the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS).
The surgeon continues the dissection medially, exposing the quadratus lumborum and psoas muscle, while clearly identifying and preserving the subcostal nerve.
The retroperitoneal fat is visibly herniating through the posterior lumbar defect, with the edges of the hernia defect clearly defined.
The surgeon cuts and removes a stitch from a prior repair, revealing a hernia defect measuring three by two centimeters.
The surgeon attempts to close the posterior lumbar defect using a 1-0 V-Loc suture, but the suture pulls through the tissue due to its friable nature.

A seven and a half by fifteen centimeter barred soft mesh is placed into the abdominal cavity, centered over the hernia defect with overlap in each direction. The mesh is secured using a 2-0 Vicryl stitch.
The surgeon inspects the mesh to confirm it lies completely flat within the posterior space, ensuring proper placement over the hernia defect.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum with a running two-oh V-lock suture, covering the previously exposed area.
The final visualization shows the completed repair with the mesh lying flat and secured over the defect in the abdominal cavity.
